1.1. Quantum metric spaces. Noncommutative geometry centers around the idea of generalizing
geometric and topological notions to the setting of noncommutative C*-algebras. One of these
structures is that of metric spaces. There have been two main approaches to define a noncommutative
notion of metric spaces:
Lipschitz seminorms approach: The first approach has its roots in the work of Alain Connes [1]
where he introduced spectral triples as a noncommutative counterpart to Spin manifolds, and noted
that this structure can be used to construct a metric on the state space of the underlying C*-algebra.
Indeed, if (𝔄, H, 𝐷) is a spectral triple with 𝔄a C*-algebra represented on a Hilbert space H
and 𝐷the “Dirac operator”, an unbounded operator on H with compact resolvent, then the norm of
the commutator ∥[𝐷, 𝑎]∥has properties of the Lipschitz seminorm of 𝑎, and corresponds to the
classical case (𝐶(𝑀), 𝐿2(𝑀, 𝑆), 𝐷) of a spin manifold 𝑀. This leads to the definition of a distance
on the state space S(𝔄) of 𝔄by: 𝑑(𝜇, 𝜈) = sup{|𝜇(𝑎) −𝜈(𝑎)| : 𝑎∈𝔄, ∥[𝐷, 𝑎]∥≤1}. This approach was further axiomatized and developed by Rieffel [2], who introduced the notion
of compact quantum metric spaces considering an arbitrary Lipschitz seminorm 𝐿on a unital
C∗-algebra 𝔄. More recently, Latr´emoli`ere [3] extended this framework to locally compact quantum

metric spaces. Finding examples of compact and noncompact quantum metric spaces is an active
area of research due to the analytical condition that the distance 𝑑(𝜇, 𝜈) = sup{|𝜇(𝑎) −𝜈(𝑎)| : 𝑎∈
𝔄, ∥[𝐷, 𝑎]∥≤1} should metrize the weak* topology of the set of states (or a subset of it in the case
of locally compact quantum metric spaces).
The Quantum relations approach: A different approach to define noncommutative metric
spaces was introduced by Kuperberg and Weaver [4] using the notion of quantum relations. In this
framework, a quantum relation on a von Neumann algebra 𝑀is defined as a weak* closed operator
bimodule over its commutant 𝑀′. Kuperberg and Weaver then define a W*-quantum metric on 𝑀
as a family of weak* closed operator systems (V𝑟)𝑟≥0 satisfying properties analogous to those of
operators of propagation less than or equal to 𝑟on a metric space. This approach has as natural
examples quantum graphs (e.g. quantum expanders), and constructions related to quantum error
correction in quantum computing. These examples are quite different from the ”geometrical” ones
arising in the Lipschitz seminorm approach, and a bridge between the two approaches becomes
desirable.
In [4], starting from this definition spaces corresponding to Lip(X) the space of Lipschitz functions
have been constructed, with technical subtleties. Furthermore, a W*-quantum metric structure was
associated to spectral triples. But the link between this approach and that of Rieffel/Latr´emoli`ere
has not been fully explored.

1.2. Coarse geometry. The definition of [4] is reminiscent of that of Roe algebras in the classical
setting. These algebras are coarse invariants (they only care about the large scale structure of the
underlying space) and have been widely studied and used in relation with index theory, the Novikov
conjecture and coarse properties of the metric spaces [5] [6].
In the classical case, we thus have: • Any proper metric space is a coarse space.
• For any coarse space, and a representation of the algebra of functions on a Hilbert space,
one can associate a Roe algebra.

1.4. Summary of results. In this work we give the following answers to the questions/program
above: • Theorem 3.5: With the additional datum given by a representation on a Hilbert space, locally
compact quantum metric spaces in the sense of Latr´emoli`ere [3] give rise to W*-metric
spaces in the sense of Kuperberg and Weaver [4].
• Theorem 4.5 and 4.7: With the additional datum given by a representation on a Hilbert space,
W*-metric spaces, and thus locally compact quantum metric spaces are noncommutative
coarse spaces in the sense of [8].
• Theorems 5.2 and 5.5: In the case of classical metric spaces, the spectral Roe algebra and
the Higson compactification coincide with the classical notions.
• Section 5: New examples of locally compact quantum metric spaces are provided: coarse
disjoint unions, and almost-commutative locally compact quantum metric spaces, furthermore
almost-commutative locally compact quantum metric spaces are shown to be coarsely
equivalent to the underlying classical metric space: theorem 5.15.
• Theorem 6.5: A Higher index can be defined for locally compact quantum metric spaces,
and under further assumptions on the asymptotic behavior of commutators of elements in
these spaces, this higher index can be defined for all K-homology classes. Remarkably, this
crucially uses the fundamental characterization of locally compact quantum metric spaces
(theorem 2.9).
• Proposition 6.1: In the case of nonunital spectral triples, one can define the Higher index of
the Dirac operator without further assumptions.
• Theorem 3.16 and section 6.3.1: As a side result relevant to classical setting, we show that
relative commutant Lipschitz seminorm and finite propagation are essentially the same in
the case of classical metric spaces with finite dimension at all scales (Finite Assouad-Nagata
dimension).

Here and there after we adopt the following notation conventions for the objects considered: – H, K denote Hilbert spaces,
– 𝔄, 𝔅, 𝔐. . . denote C∗-algebras (underlying quantum metric spaces),
– 𝑋for locally compact spaces,
– 𝑀for von Neumann algebras. Along with the following notations of standard constructions on these objects: – B(H) denotes the set of bounded operators on H,
– K(H) denotes the set compact operators on H,
– 𝜎(𝔐) denotes the Gelfand spectrum of an abelian C∗-algebra 𝔐,
– 𝔰𝔞(𝔄) denotes the self-adjoint part of a C∗-algebra 𝔄,
– 𝔲𝔄denotes the unitization of a C∗-algebra 𝔄,
– M(𝔄) denotes the multiplier algebra of a C∗-algebra 𝔄.

2.1. Locally Compact Quantum Metric Spaces. The notion of compact quantum metric spaces
was defined, based on the formula of the distance between probability measures on compact metric
spaces due to Kantorovich, and considered by Connes and by Rieffel in [2]. This notion was later
extended to the locally compact case by Latr´emoli`ere in [3]. We present here this definition of locally
compact quantum metric spaces. As we will be dealing mainly with proper quantum metric spaces,
all the algebras 𝔄and 𝔐involved in this section and thereafter will be assumed to be separable.
Quantum metric spaces (at least in their C*-version) are defined by equipping a C∗∗-algebra with a
Lipschitz seminorms. Indeed in the classical case, the Lipschitz seminorms recover the distance.

Definition 2.1 (Lipschitz pair). A Lipschitz pair (𝔄, 𝐿) is given by a C∗-algebra 𝔄and a seminorm
𝐿defined on a dense subspace dom(𝐿) of the self-adjoint part of the unitization 𝔰𝔞(𝔲𝔄) of 𝔄, such
that: {𝑎∈𝔰𝔞(𝔲𝔄) : 𝐿(𝑎) = 0} = R1𝔲𝔄

The seminorm 𝐿generalizes the notion of the Lipschitz seminorm for functions on a metric space.
Given a Lipschitz pair (𝔄, 𝐿), one can define a metric on the state space S(𝔄) of 𝔄by:
mk𝐿(𝜇, 𝜈) = sup{|𝜇(𝑎) −𝜈(𝑎)| | 𝑎∈𝔰𝔞(𝔲𝔄), 𝐿(𝑎) ≤1}

Looking at the classical case, while for compact metric spaces, this metric metrizes the weak*
topology on the state space, for locally compact metric spaces this is no longer true in general.
This is because, in some sense, the fact that measures can escape to infinity prevents sequential
compactness. Instead, Dobrushin [10] shows that if one restricts to sets of measures satisfying a
tightness condition, then the Monge-Kantorovich metric metrizes the weak* topology on these sets:

Theorem 2.2 (Dobrushin [10]). If S is a set of Borel probability measures on a locally compact
metric space (𝑋, 𝑑) such that for some 𝑥0 ∈𝑋: lim
𝑟→∞sup{

metric metrizes the weak* topology on the state space, or equivalently that the unit ball of the
Lipschitz seminorm is totally bounded for the quotient norm on 𝔰𝔞(𝔄)/R1.

Definition 2.3 (quantum compact metric spaces [2]). A quantum compact metric space is a unital
Lipschitz pair (𝔄, 𝐿) such that the Monge-Kantorovich metric mk𝐿metrizes the weak* topology on
the state space S(𝔄).

It was shown in [2] that this definition is equivalent to the total boundedness of the unit ball of the
Lipschitz seminorm for the quotient norm on 𝔰𝔞(𝔄)/R1.
Similarly, in [3] locally compact quantum metric spaces were required to satisfy the condition that
the Monge-Kantorovich metric metrizes the weak* topology on certain subsets of the state space
that generalize the above Dobrushin tightness condition. To this end, the notion of topography was
introduced to play the role of specifying a notion lim𝑟→∞∗= 0. Definition 2.4 (Lipschitz triple). A Lipschitz triple is a triple (𝔄, 𝔐, 𝐿) where: • (𝔄, 𝐿) is a Lipschitz pair,
• 𝔐is an abelian C∗-subalgebra of 𝔄, called the topography, such that 𝔐contains an
approximate unit for 𝔄,

The topography 𝔐plays the role of specifying which elements of 𝔄vanish at infinity, and allows
one to define local states.

Definition 2.5 (Local states [3]). We introduce the following: • A state 𝜇∈S(𝔄) is a local state supported in 𝐾⊂𝜎(𝔐) compact if 𝜇(𝜒𝐾) = 1. If this
condition is satisfied we shall say that 𝜇is in S(𝔄|𝐾).
• The set of local states is denoted by S(𝔄|𝔐): S(𝔄|𝔐) =

S(𝔄|𝐾). We define tame subsets of the state space S(𝔄) that generalize the Dobrushin tightness condition.

Definition 2.6 (Tame subsets of the state space [3]). A subset S ⊆S(𝔄) is tame if for some (hence
any) local state 𝜇∈S(𝔄|𝔐) we have: lim
𝐾∈K(𝜎(𝔐)) sup{|𝜑(𝑎−𝜒𝐾𝑎𝜒𝐾)| : 𝜑∈S, 𝑎∈𝔰𝔞(𝔄), 𝐿(𝑎) ≤1, 𝜇(𝑎) = 0} = 0. With these notions we can now define locally compact quantum metric spaces.

Definition 2.7 (locally compact quantum metric spaces [3]). A Lipschitz triple (𝔄, 𝔐, 𝐿) is called
a locally compact quantum metric space if the Monge-Kantorovich metric mk𝐿metrizes the weak*
topology on any tame subset of the state space S(𝔄).

Remark 2.8. Note that for any compact 𝐾in 𝜎(𝔐), the set of local states S(𝔄|𝐾) is tame. Thus
the Monge-Kantorovich metric metrizes the weak* topology on S(𝔄|𝐾), in particular: diam(S(𝔄|𝐾), mk𝐿) < ∞. Lipschitz triples satisfying this condition are called regular.

Generalizing the characterization of compact quantum metric spaces in [2], [3] provides a
characterization of locally compact quantum metric spaces in terms of total boundedness of certain
sets.

Theorem 2.9 (See [3]). A Lipschitz triple (𝔄, 𝔐, 𝐿) is a locally compact quantum metric space if
and only if one of the following equivalent conditions holds: • For all local states 𝜇∈S(𝔄|𝔐) and for all compactly supported 𝑠, 𝑡∈𝔐, the set: {𝑠𝑎𝑡| 𝑎∈𝔰𝔞(𝔲𝔄), 𝐿(𝑎) ≤1, 𝜇(𝑎) = 0}

is totally bounded for the norm topology of 𝔄.
• There exists a local state 𝜇∈S(𝔄|𝔐) such that for all compactly supported 𝑠, 𝑡∈𝔐, the
set:
{𝑠𝑎𝑡| 𝑎∈𝔰𝔞(𝔲𝔄), 𝐿(𝑎) ≤1, 𝜇(𝑎) = 0} is totally bounded for the norm topology of 𝔄.
• (only if 𝔄is separable) There exists a local state 𝜇∈S(𝔄|𝔐) and a strictly positive element
ℎ∈𝔐such that the set: {ℎ𝑎ℎ| 𝑎∈𝔰𝔞(𝔲𝔄), 𝐿(𝑎) ≤1, 𝜇(𝑎) = 0}
is totally bounded for the norm topology of 𝔄.

Remark 2.10. In particular all the Lipschitz triples in this paper will be assumed to be separable
(since we will be working with proper quantum metric spaces, see below).
Recall that in the classical case, Lipschitz seminorms satisfy the Leibniz condition:
𝐿( 𝑓𝑔) ≤∥𝑓∥𝐿(𝑔) + 𝐿( 𝑓)∥𝑔∥. This condition has been considered in the quantum case as well.

Definition 2.11 (Leibniz Lipschitz pair [7]). A Leibniz Lipschitz pair is a Lipschitz pair (𝔄, 𝐿)
such that: • the domain dom(𝐿) is a Jordan-Lie subalgebra of 𝔰𝔞(𝔲𝔄) i.e. it is closed under the Jordan
bracket 𝑎◦𝑏= 𝑎𝑏+𝑏𝑎

metric space (𝔄, 𝔐, 𝐿) is said to be proper if • 𝔄is separable,
• (𝔄, 𝐿) is a Leibniz Lipschitz pair,
• 𝐿is lower semi-continuous with respect to the norm topology on 𝔰𝔞(𝔄),
• 𝔏𝔬𝔠(𝔄, ∗) ∩dom(𝐿) is norm dense in dom(𝐿),
• 𝔏𝔬𝔠(𝔄, ∗) ∩dom(𝐿) ∩𝔐is norm dense in 𝔐,
• (𝜎(𝔐), mk𝐿|𝔐) induced by (𝔐, 𝐿|𝔐) is a proper metric space, i.e., all closed balls are
compact. (note that 𝐿|𝔐is densely defined on 𝔐by the conditions above). A more restrictive, easier to deal with notion of properness, called strong properness, was also
introduced in [3].

Definition 2.13 (strongly proper quantum metric spaces [3]). A locally compact quantum metric
space (𝔄, 𝔐, 𝐿) is said to be strongly proper if: • 𝐿can be extended to a dense subset of 𝔄, such that: 𝐿(𝑎𝑏) ≤∥𝑎∥𝐿(𝑏) + 𝐿(𝑎)∥𝑏∥. • 𝔄is separable and 𝐿is lower semi-continuous,
• {𝑚∈𝔰𝔞(𝔐) | 𝐿(𝑚) < ∞},
• there exists an approximate unit (𝑒𝑛)𝑛∈N in 𝔐for 𝔄such that for all 𝑛∈N, we have
∥𝑒𝑛∥≤1, 𝑒𝑛∈𝜒𝐾𝑛𝔄𝜒𝐾𝑛for some compact 𝐾𝑛⊂𝜎(𝔐), and lim𝑛→∞𝐿(𝑒𝑛) = 0. 2.2. Noncommutative coarse geometry. Definition 2.14 (noncommutative coarse geometry [8]). A noncommutative coarse space is a pair
(𝔄, 𝔄) where 𝔄is a 𝐶∗-algebra and 𝔄is a unital 𝐶∗-subalgebra of the multiplier algebra M(𝔄)
containing 𝔄.

Remark 2.15. The datum above is equivalent to the datum of a unital 𝐶∗-algebra 𝔄of which 𝔄is an
essential ideal.

In the above, the algebra 𝔄plays the role of the algebra of “slowly oscillating functions” and
generalizes the Higson compactification in the commutative case. The pair encodes the large-scale
(coarse) geometry of the noncommutative space. We recall below the precise statements in the
motivating classical case, we refer to [11] and [8] for more details.

Definition 2.16 (Higson compactification). For 𝑋a proper coarse space: • The algebra of slowly oscillating functions is given by: 𝐶ℎ(𝑋) = { 𝑓∈𝐶𝑏(𝑋) : d 𝑓vanishes at infinity on controlled sets}. where d 𝑓(𝑥, 𝑦) = 𝑓(𝑥) −𝑓(𝑦) for all (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈𝑋× 𝑋.
• The spectrum of 𝐶ℎ(𝑋) is called the Higson compactification of 𝑋and is denoted by ℎ𝑋.
• In the case of proper metric spaces (𝑋, 𝑑), this definition can be made more explicit, and
one has:
𝐶ℎ(𝑋) = { 𝑓∈𝐶𝑏(𝑋) :∀𝑅> 0, ∀𝜀> 0, ∃𝐾⊂𝑋compact, ∀𝑥, 𝑦∉𝐾, 𝑑(𝑥, 𝑦) ≤𝑅⇒| 𝑓(𝑥) −𝑓(𝑦)| ≤𝜀}.

Furthermore if one starts from a compactification 𝑋of a locally compact space 𝑋, one can define
a coarse structure on 𝑋, denoted by 𝑡𝑋, by declaring a subset 𝐸⊂𝑋× 𝑋to be controlled if and
only if nets in 𝐸, (𝑥𝑖, 𝑦𝑖) converge to 𝜕𝑋only mutually, i.e. if 𝑥𝑖→𝜔∈𝜕𝑋then 𝑦𝑖→𝜔as well.
It is then shown in [11], [8] that these two constructions are inverse of each other in the case of
proper metric spaces, i.e. starting from a proper metric space 𝑋, the coarse structure induced by the
Higson compactification coincides with the coarse structure coming from the metric, and starting
from a compactification 𝑋of a locally compact space 𝑋, the Higson compactification of the coarse
structure induced by 𝑋coincides with 𝑋. This motivates the noncommutative definition relying on
fixing an analogue to the algebra of slowly oscillating functions.
Although the two constructions are in some sense equivalent to each other, the coarse structures
obtained from considering a compactified topological space (with respect to some natural compacti-
fication, for instance if one considers a manifold with boundary), and those obtained from the metric
coarse structure can be very different. For instance, already for R with its usual metric, the Higson
compactification associated with the metric coarse structure is not second countable. If one has a
locally compact Riemannian manifold 𝑀that can be viewed as the interior of a compact manifold
with boundary 𝑀, then the coarse structure induced by the compactification 𝑀is in general different
from the metric coarse structure. The interplay between the two can lead to interesting results, in
index theory in particular, see for instance [12].
Example 2.17 (The Euclidean plane). Consider R2, then one obtains two coarse structures: • The metric coarse structure coming from the usual Euclidean metric.

In this paper we take a different approach since we start from a quantum metric space, in particular
we are motivated by the case of the Moyal plane, where we can start from the quantum metric
structure constructed in [3], rather than from a Rieffel deformation, this is reminiscient of the metric
coarse structure on R2. Comparing with the “topological coarse struture” on the Moyal plane
considered in [8] in order to conceptualize some index computations on the Moyal plane would be
an interesting question for future research.

2.3. W*-quantum metric spaces. Kuperberg and Weaver in [4] introduced a notion of quantum
metric spaces in the setting of von Neumann algebras, inspired by the notion of finite propagation
operators. We recall here some of their definitions.

Definition 2.18 (W*-filtration [4]). A W*-filtration of B(H) is a family V = (V𝑟)𝑟≥0 of dual
operator systems (i.e. weak* closed self-adjoint unital subspaces) in B(H) such that: (1) ∀𝑟, 𝑠≥0, V𝑟· V𝑠⊆V𝑟+𝑠,
(2) ∀𝑟≥0, V𝑟= Ñ
𝑠>𝑟V𝑠. Definition 2.19 (W*-quantum metric space [4]). A W*-quantum pseudometric space is a von
Neumann algebra M ⊂B(H) such that B(H) has a W*-filtration V = (V𝑟)𝑟≥0 such that M′ ⊆V0.
If in addition M′ = V0, then we say that (M, V) is a W*-quantum metric space.

The intuition is that the elements of V𝑟are operators of propagation at most 𝑟. In the commutative
discrete case, if 𝑋is a discrete metric space with metric 𝑑, and H = 𝑙2(𝑋), then one can define: V𝑑
𝑟= {𝑇∈B(𝑙2(𝑋)) : 𝑑(𝑥, 𝑦) > 𝑟⇒⟨𝑇𝛿𝑥, 𝛿𝑦⟩= 0}. Then (𝑙∞(𝑋), V𝑑) is a W*-quantum metric space. V0 are operator of propagation 0, i.e. multiplica-
tion operators.

If M is represented on two Hilbert spaces H1 and H2, then [4] shows that there is 1-1
correspondence between W*-filtrations on B(H1) and B(H2) making (M, V(1)) and (M, V(2))
W*-quantum metric spaces. Thus the notion of W*-quantum metric space does not depend on the
representation.
Furthermore, [4] defines the following notion of reflexivity for W*-quantum metric spaces.

Definition 2.20 (Reflexive W*-quantum metric space [4]). A W*-quantum metric space (M, V) is
said to be reflexive if for all 𝑟≥0, V𝑟is reflexive as a dual operator space, i.e. : V𝑟= {𝐵∈B(H) | 𝑃V𝑟𝑄= 0 =⇒𝑃𝐵𝑄= 0}

Noting that projections in M can be endowed with a notion of distance coming from the W*-
filtration, the definition is useful in that it allows one to recover the W*-filtration from the distance
between projections of M.

5.1. The commutative case. In this section, we verify that in the case of locally compact metric
spaces, the spectral Roe algebra corresponds to the usual Roe algebra and the spectral Higson
compactification defined above coincides with the usual Higson compactification for proper metric
spaces.

5.4. Almost-commutative Lipschitz triples. Riemannian manifolds and in particular spin mani-
folds provide a rich source of metric spaces. In noncommutative geometry, the counterpart, and
one of the motivating examples for C*-quantum metric spaces, are spectral triples. In this context,
some of the simplest examples after commutative spectral triples (i.e. spin manifolds), which led to
models for the Standard Model of particle physics, are almost-commutative spectral triples, which
are tensor products of commutative spectral triples with finite-dimensional spectral triples. That is,
triples of the form (𝐶∞(𝑀) ˆ⊗𝔄𝐹, 𝐿2(𝑀, 𝑆) ˆ⊗H𝐹, /𝐷ˆ⊗1 + 𝜖ˆ⊗𝐷𝐹) where • 𝑀is a compact spin manifold, 𝑆the spinor bundle over 𝑀, /𝐷the associated Dirac operator,
and 𝜖the (constant) graduation of 𝐿2(𝑀, 𝑆) in the even case (even spinors are +1 eigenvectors,
and odd spinors are −1), and 𝜖= 1 in the odd case,
• 𝔄𝐹is a finite dimensional C*-algebra,
• H𝐹is a finite dimensional Hilbert space,
• 𝐷𝐹is a self-adjoint operator on H𝐹, making (𝔄𝐹, H𝐹, 𝐷𝐹) a finite dimensional spectral
triple. In this model the algebra 𝔄𝐹encodes the internal degrees of freedom of the elementary particles,
while the manifold 𝑀encodes the spacetime. Since we are interested in the metric coarse structure
we shall consider models inspired by these construction where: • 𝑀is a complete Riemannian manifold (not necessarily compact).
• We focus on the quantum metric structure (and forget about other structures carried by the
spinor bundle and the Dirac operator). In this context, to motivate the right class of Lipschitz seminorms to consider on 𝐶0(𝑀) ˆ⊗𝔄𝐹, we
note that all Lipschitz seminorms on finite dimensional C*-algebras are equivalent (in the sense that
they are comparable up to multiplicative constants), since the formula inf{∥𝑎−𝑐1 | for 𝑐∈C∥}

In this section, we fix a proper locally compact quantum metric space (𝔄, 𝔐, 𝐿) represented
non-degenerately and amply on a Hilbert space H. Using our definition of Roe algebras, we deduce
some aspects of Higher index theory for locally compact quantum metric spaces.
As operators of finite spectral propagation with respect to the full algebra are difficult to construct
in general we shall consider the following two types of Higher index: • The first version is valued in the relative commutant Roe algebras 𝐶∗
comm(𝔄) defined in
section 3, for which one can use the functoriality properties. (Note that the larger 𝐶∗
𝑞(𝔄) in
the commutative case has been used to define the Higher index of some pseudodifferential
operators). While this Higher index can be defined in general for Dirac operators coming
from spectral triples, in order to define it for general elements of the K-homology of 𝔄, we
shall need to assume the existence of a partition of unity that commutes sufficiently well
with elements of 𝔄.
• The second version is valued in the spectral Roe algebra with respect to an arbitrary
commutative subalgebra 𝔐⊂𝔅⊂𝔄, (for instance 𝔅= 𝔐) for which one can construct
finite propagation operators by the usual cutting procedure. We first introduce the dual algebras 𝐷∗

some assumptions on the regularity of commutants in 𝔄with respect to the Lipschitz norm,

6.2. Lipschitz controlled K-theory and quantitative K-theory, a comparison for finite Assouad-
Nagata dimension. In this brief section, we note that the above construction of localized relative
commutant Roe algebras, along with theorem 3.16, allows to compare two different approaches to
approximate K-homology of metric spaces, namely quantitative K-theory [17] [18] and Lipschitz
controlled K-theory [19]. With the filtration given by the propagation prop, quantitative K-theory of
the Roe algebra was used to approximate K-homology. While Lipschitz controlled K-theory [19]
was mainly used to approximate K-theory of metric spaces, but if we instead consider the Lipschitz
controlled K-theory for the relative commutant algebras introduced above, with Lipschitz seminorm
𝐿∗, and in the case of metric spaces of finite Assouad-Nagata dimension, then this is equivalent to
using quantitative K-theory to approximate the K-homology of the space as in [18] for instance.
We invite the interested reader to refer to [19] and [17] [18] for the definitions of these two notions
of controlled K-theory, the result then follows immediately from theorem 3.16.

In this paper we have established a link between locally compact quantum metric spaces and
noncommutative coarse geometry, furthermore we have defined notions of Roe algebras and higher
index theory in this framework. We believe that we have only scrached the surface of this new
connection, and there are many open questions and directions that we leave for future research, in
particular in connection with other developments in other fields of noncommutative geometry. We
outline some of these directions below.

7.1. More examples of spectral Roe algebra computations. One direction is to compute the
spectral Roe algebra for more examples of locally compact quantum metric spaces with more
fundamental noncommutativity. One promising example is the Moyal plane, with the spectral triple
strucuture defined on it in [21] and considered as locally compact quantum metric space as in [3].
We expect that in such cases, the relative commutant Roe algebra and the spectral Roe algebra will
differ significantly. Indeed the relative commutant Roe algebra, along with the noncommutative
coarse structure arising from it, looks to be more natural in the noncommutative setting.

7.2. Geometric realization of (finite) W*-quantum metric spaces. In relation with the point
above, it would be interesting to study, even in the simplest cases (for instance M𝑛(C) with a
Lipschitz seminorm), what kind of W* quantum metric structure arise from the spectral propagation,
and from the spetral propagation with respect to the diagonal subalgebra C𝑛, furthermore one could
study what happens when different representations of M𝑛(C) are considered. As arbitrary quantum
graphs (and in particular those giving rise to quantum expanders) can be naturally viewed in the
framework of quantum relations and thus of W* quantum metric spaces, this could allow us to

construct “geometric realizations” of quantum graphs (and in particular quantum expanders) in the
sense of Lipschitz seminorms.

7.3. Spectral triples. In [4], W* quantum metric structures were associated to spectral triples,
roughly by declaring that 𝑒𝑖𝑡𝐷has propagation |𝑡|. In our construction, we have shown that locally
compact quantum metric spaces with a faithful representation give rise to W* quantum metric
spaces. It would be interesting to compare these two constructions, and see if they agree in some
cases. Again we expect this to fail in general and that the relative commutant Roe algebra is the
closer concept.

7.4. Index theory. While we have defined two types of higher indices for locally compact quantum
metric spaces, and in parallel with the classical case, it would be interesting to study more concrete
examples and compute these indices in some cases. The Moyal plane appears to be a promising
example in this direction as well.

7.5. 𝐿∗seminorms. The seminorm 𝐿∗satisfies basic properties that make it a good candidate for a
noncommutative Lipschitz seminorm, aside from the fact that 𝐿∗(𝑇) = 0 does not imply that 𝑇is a
scalar multiple of the identity. For finite dimensional algebras represented irreducibly on a finite
dimensional Hilbert H𝐹space, this issue does not arise, and one gets a Lipschitz seminorm on
B(H𝐹). It would be interesting to study this seminorm in more detail, and see if it can be used to
construct new examples of (finite) quantum metric spaces, or if it can be used to give new insights
on existing examples. For more general, nonfinite spaces, one can ask if one can take subspaces or
quotient spaces of the relative commutant Roe algebra where 𝐿∗is a Lipschitz seminorm, and see if
this can lead to new examples of quantum metric spaces.
